questions.questionText: Pott's disease (spinal tuberculosis) most commonly affects which region of the spine?
questions.optionB: Lower thoracic and thoracolumbar junction (T10-L2 — anterior vertebral body involvement causing collapse, kyphosis/gibbus deformity, cold abscess)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Spinal TB = Pott's disease = lower thoracic/thoracolumbar junction most common. Why correct: TB spine: anterior vertebral body paradiscal lesion → disc space narrowing → vertebral collapse → angular kyphosis (gibbus). Cold abscess (psoas abscess tracking to groin). Most common site: lower thoracic/thoracolumbar (T10-L2). Why A wrong: Cervical TB — less common but more dangerous (quadriplegia risk). Why C wrong: Sacral involvement is uncommon. Why D wrong: Upper cervical (atlantoaxial) — rare. Clinical relevance: X-ray: paradiscal erosion, reduced disc space, vertebral collapse. MRI: best imaging. Cold abscess: psoas or retropharyngeal. Treatment: ATT (anti-tubercular therapy) for 12-18 months. Surgery for neurological deficit/instability. Exam trap: TB = ANTERIOR body + disc destruction; metastases = POSTERIOR elements spared + disc preserved. This differentiates TB from tumor. Memory aid: 'Pott's = Posterior elements spared, Anterior body attacked, Thoraco-Lumbar.' Reference: Maheshwari – Spine.
questions.conceptTag: Pott disease spinal TB thoracolumbar gibbus cold abscess
